title: The Supreme Court Killed the Voting Rights Act Over Doctored Data
narrative: Justice Samuel Alito's Callais v. US ruling gutted the Voting Rights Act, citing a Justice Department filing that claimed Black voter turnout exceeded white voter turnout in two of the last five presidential elections. DOJ used this to argue Section 2 is 'broad enough already' — that the law didn't need to protect against racial discrimination in voting because racial discrimination in voting wasn't happening. Alito called the data 'instructive.' The Supreme Court of the United States used it to eliminate a key protection.
telemetry: Guardian (May 8): exclusive analysis — the DoJ data cited in the filing has 'questionable methodology' and does not hold up under independent scrutiny
Above The Law (May): the DoJ claim is 'fake facts in a Supreme Court opinion' — not advocacy, a direct description of the judicial record
NPR (May 2): the DoJ filing was the empirical backbone of the Callais ruling — without it, the legal argument collapses

Census Bureau / Current Population Survey (CPS) data: white voter turnout consistently exceeded Black voter turnout in every presidential election from 2000-2020 — the DoJ filing appears to have inverted or misread the numbers
Legal scholars on X: the CPS data is publicly available, the error is not subtle, and the Court's own researchers should have caught it
The ruling was 6-3, with Alito writing for the majority — this was the deciding vote on the factual assertion
analysis: The error here isn't subtle. CPS voter turnout data is one of the most analyzed datasets in American political science. If Black turnout exceeded white turnout in 2008 and 2012 — as the DoJ filing claims — that would be a historically significant finding requiring explanation. It would also be false. The CPS data shows white turnout higher in both years. Someone at DoJ either inverted the numbers, misread a table, or submitted something they knew was wrong. The Supreme Court, which has clerks and researchers and access to the same public data, cited it as 'instructive' without flagging the discrepancy. The result is a ruling that eliminates a major civil rights protection on the basis of a number that is wrong by a structurally significant margin. This isn't a drafting error. This is the mechanism: a false empirical claim was the load-bearing pillar of a legal argument that erased a constitutional protection.
verdict: [SIPHONED VERDICT]: The Court killed the Voting Rights Act on fabricated data. Whether it was DOJ sloppiness or deliberate manipulation, the outcome is the same — a 6-3 majority opinion built on a number that doesn't survive contact with the census.
